methodology: All 33 attempts were judged from the same five captured scenes against the supplied real-crayon samples. Scores use the fixed 100-point rubric: wax/body 25, fine natural tooth 20, contained crisp edge 15, same-hue buildup (including continuous no-lift retrace) 25, color interaction 10, and full-drawing quality 5. capture.json activation evidence was checked for every attempt; flat default output was treated as a likely plain-pen fallback, while a default capture was not penalized merely for lacking a setter when its pixels visibly demonstrated texture. Rank 1 is worst and rank 33 is best.

attempts.rationale: The activated waxy variant is extremely pale and translucent, reading as a fading highlighter rather than wax. Retracing mostly changes opacity and the full drawing nearly disappears.
Activation succeeded, but enormous random-looking voids break the stroke into confetti. It is contained, yet has neither a dense wax body nor convincing gradual buildup.
The selected crayon is drastically under-filled with coarse holes, so the house looks eroded rather than drawn in wax. Repeated passes remain patchy instead of filling fine tooth naturally.
The crayon style activates but uses large conspicuous gaps and an uneven digital pattern. It improves slightly with passes, though the sparse body and crossing treatment remain unlike real wax.
The stroke is contained but dominated by coarse punched-out holes, especially in the single line and full drawing. Buildup exists, but the base deposit is too discontinuous to read as a crayon body.
Visible tooth and pass filling are present, but the holes are much coarser than the reference and create a chipped-paint appearance. Crossings mostly stack rather than mix.
This activated brush has a usable dense core, but isolated large white voids look synthetic rather than like fine paper tooth. Continuous and lifted buildup are present but visually uneven.
capture.json shows only default activation and the result is a smooth, flat marker. The body and edges are clean, but there is essentially no paper tooth or wax-specific buildup.
The default capture is visually indistinguishable from the standard solid pen. It makes a tidy drawing, but misses the defining tooth, deposit, and mixing behavior.
Although the waxy setter was invoked, the screenshots remain nearly flat and marker-like. There is little perceptible texture or distinction between repeated deposits.
The selected variant produces a dense but overwhelmingly uniform line. Its clean containment helps, yet its texture and buildup cues are too weak to match the references.
This is another mostly solid marker result despite successful activation. Repeated passes add modest density, but do not visibly fill a convincing tooth pattern.
The crayon setter works, but the rendered line is smooth enough to read as a pen. Subtle modulation is visible at close range, though buildup and color crossings remain conventional.
A solid wax-colored body is present, but fine tooth is almost absent. The no-lift and lifted scenes show too little material change to sell progressive wax deposit.
The variant is well-contained and usable, but resembles an opaque marker in every scene. Slight pass variation raises it above the flattest attempts, without reaching real crayon texture.
The stroke has faint internal modulation but still reads primarily as flat paint. Its edge and finished drawing are clean; buildup and interaction are under-expressed.
Two-octave variation is too restrained in these captures, leaving a marker-like result. It maintains hue and containment, but overlap does little visible tooth filling.

This activated blue-noise brush is clean and dense but visually close to the standard pen at artifact scale. Buildup is somewhat clearer than lower-ranked flat variants, while texture remains too quiet.
The paper-tooth variant has subtle fine texture, but the body is so uniform that it still reads as a softened marker. It reproduces a pleasant full drawing without strong wax buildup.
Successful activation yields slight texture and excellent containment, but the single line and retrace remain visually flat. The result is polished yet insufficiently crayon-specific.
A restrained, fine-grained texture begins to read as wax and does not escape the path. Repeated passes densify modestly, but the effect is weaker than the supplied buildup references.
The stroke has gentle tonal and edge variation and retains a dense body. Tooth remains faint, though lifted passes show a more credible same-hue fill-in than the marker-like group.

Clearly visible texture and buildup distinguish this from flat pens, but the isolated holes are too large and harsh. Crossings preserve texture enough to suggest physical media, though not true mixing.
The brush deposits a credible dense layer and fills visible gaps on repeated passes. Its tooth is still coarse and digitally spotty compared with the real samples, limiting naturalism.

Although capture.json used the default path, the pixels visibly show fine, contained texture rather than a plain fallback. It looks crayon-like in single strokes, but buildup changes are less convincing than the leaders.
Fine texture is evenly contained in a strong core and produces an attractive complete drawing. Pass-to-pass filling is visible but comparatively restrained, and crossings mostly overpaint.
This has a convincing fine screen of paper tooth, crisp broken edges, and clear densification in the multi-pass rows. It is slightly too regular and does little special at color crossings.
The fine texture and dense core are well balanced, creating a natural dry-wax surface without spray. Buildup is credible, though quieter than the best attempts and still source-over at crossings.
A fine, evenly distributed tooth closely resembles wax on paper and gradually fills while retaining hue. Edges are slightly too smooth and crossings show limited pigment interaction.

The default crayon has a dense wax body, visible fine tooth, and strong same-hue densification across repeated passes. Some larger voids and a somewhat uniform pattern keep it below the most natural candidates.

Fine contained tooth remains visible in the first pass and fills convincingly in both retrace tests without muddying hue. The full drawing reads strongly as crayon, though crossings still mostly layer.

This has the richest wax body and especially convincing irregular tooth in the house and single line. It builds density strongly at constant hue, with only slightly dark mottling and ordinary source-over crossings holding it back.

The best overall balance: fine crisp tooth, a dense but visibly broken wax body, strict containment, and the clearest progressive fill-in from single through repeated passes at stable hue. Cross-color behavior remains mostly layered, leaving room for the next iteration.
